one_line_takeaway: Feeding a code LLM's own hallucinated output back into a retriever to fetch project-specific API references and re-prompting (iterative grounding) beats plain RAG and consistently cuts API hallucinations across five models and two tasks, with no model fine-tuning.
key_finding: Iterative grounding consistently and significantly improves generation across all five models. For code completion (Table 3), the iterative prompt relatively improves edit distance by 23.3-50.6%, normalized edit similarity by 12.1-27.5%, and exact API match by 23.9-61.0% over the baseline (no-retrieval initial prompt); the iterative prompt beats plain RAG on every model/metric (e.g., UniXcoder edit distance drops from 52.4 baseline to 46.8 RAG to 25.9 iterative). For test generation (Table 4), the iterative variant raises passing tests by 17.9%, statement coverage by 15.5%, and fixed hallucinated tests by 63.2% over TestPilot. The preliminary study found API hallucination affects 44% of all completion tasks (59% of tasks where the LLM alone fails) and causes ~16.4% of TestPilot-generated tests to crash.
core_contribution: De-Hallucinator is a black-box technique that reduces API 'hallucinations' by off-the-shelf code LLMs by iteratively grounding their prompts with project-specific API references. Its distinguishing idea over standard RAG is the 'iterative prompt': rather than retrieving context only from the initial prompt, De-Hallucinator feeds the model's OWN previous (often hallucinated) completion back into a retriever to fetch increasingly relevant project API references, adds them as commented '# API Reference:' lines, and re-queries the model up to k times. The paper contributes (1) an empirical preliminary study quantifying how often API hallucination causes code-generation failures, (2) the iterative-grounding algorithm itself, and (3) an evaluation across two tasks (Python code completion, JavaScript test generation), two languages, and five LLMs. Crucially, the model is never fine-tuned or trained; retrieval uses off-the-shelf sentence/code embedding models (SentenceBERT/code variant for completion, a Jina BERT code model for test generation) indexed in a Ball Tree.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a software-engineering / code-generation reliability paper. 'Hallucination' here means factual incorrectness of generated code (referring to non-existent project APIs or reimplementing existing ones), not an adversarial or security threat. There is no attacker, no trust boundary, and no security property under evaluation. The authors themselves frame it as a code-completion quality and developer-productivity problem.
limitations: (1) De-Hallucinator assumes the API already exists at generation time; if a developer writes a usage before implementing the API, retrieval finds nothing and the method gives no benefit (authors suggest a similarity-threshold abstention). (2) Conclusions are validated only for Python and JavaScript, though the approach is language-agnostic in principle. (3) Project sample may not be representative despite diversity efforts. (4) The completion benchmark is synthetic (lines deleted from real code with ground truth = original), and cases the base model already nails are excluded, which measures improvement on the harder residual rather than end-to-end accuracy. (5) Exact-API-match absolute numbers are low (e.g., 5.9-13.4 out of the task set), so large relative gains sit on a small base. (6) Local LLM latency is high (up to 66.7s/query for CodeGen v2.5 on a T4), mitigated only by appeal to production GPU clusters.

datasets_used: 11 Python GitHub projects for code completion (graphene, geopy, thefuck, websockets, arrow, lektor, streamparse, supervisor, seaborn, black, scikit-learn), yielding 11 projects x 10 tasks x 4 models = 440 code-completion tasks
12 CommonJS JavaScript GitHub projects for test generation (node-red, winston, prettier, commander.js, js-sdsl, howler.js, ws, handlebars.js, bluebird, joi, pm2, eleventy)
Preliminary study: 50 function-level Python completion tasks (10 projects x 5 functions, functions <=25 lines) plus TestPilot-generated tests on 12 JavaScript projects
quantitative_results: Code completion edit distance (lower better): iterative prompt gives 25.9 (UniXcoder, -50.6% vs 52.4 baseline), 30.7 (CodeGen v1, -23.3% vs 40.0), 30.1 (CodeGen v2.5, -36.3% vs 47.2), 33.5 (StarCoder+, -24.9% vs 44.6)
Code completion exact API match (higher better): iterative gives 5.9 (UniXcoder, +23.9%), 11.1 (CodeGen v1, +55.3%), 13.4 (CodeGen v2.5, +61.0%), 7.5 (StarCoder+, +32.0%) vs baselines 4.8/7.1/8.3/5.7
Code completion normalized edit similarity improvements 12.1-27.5% over baseline

Test generation (Table 4): passing tests 76.4 iterative (+17.9%) vs 64.8 TestPilot; coverage 37.0 (+15.5%) vs 32.1; fixed hallucinations 31.4 iterative (+63.2%) and 43.2 for RAG&iterative combined (+94.0%) vs 19.3
Preliminary study: API hallucination affects 44% (22/50) of completion tasks and 59% of the 37 tasks where the LLM fails; 16.4% of TestPilot tests crash with 'is not a function'/'undefined' errors
Of 622 iterative-prompt instances in test generation, 16.7% yield a passing test and 26.8% yield a non-hallucinating test
Hyperparameters: k=3 iterations default; n=20 API references for completion (exact-match peaks n=10-20), n=3 for test generation (coverage peaks at n=3)

Efficiency: CodeQL pre-analysis <1s per 1000 LoC (<=80s, mostly <=26s per project); retrieval+prompt construction 21-227ms (completion), 0.1-17ms (test gen); LLM query 1.3s (GPT-3.5) to 66.7s (CodeGen v2.5 on T4)
baselines_compared: Code completion baseline: 'initial prompt' = each LLM queried with preceding code only, no retrieval (2,048-token prompt, truncated from start), across CodeGen 2.7B, CodeGen 2.5 7B, UniXcoder 125M, StarCoder+ 15.5B
Intermediate baseline: 'RAG prompt' = retrieval from the initial prompt only (standard RAG), which the iterative variant is shown to beat
Test generation baseline: original TestPilot with GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 (1h/package, 130k token limit, 4 completions/prompt, temperature 0.1)
